Paper Name: A System for Predicting Hospital Admission at Emergency Department Based on Electronic Health Record Using Convolution Neural Network
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: 2D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) applied to structured tabular records by utilizing a non-image data-to-image transformation pipeline.• Modules Stack: Data pre-processing (cleansing and feature indexing), random under-sampling coupled with class weight balancing, 1D-to-2D feature mapping generating 120×120×3 input matrices.• Network Blocks: Four 2D convolutional layers with 4×1 kernels, max-pooling layers (2×2), Rectified Linear Units (ReLU), a dropout regularization module (0.5 rate), and a Sigmoid activation output layer.• Tech Stack: Python, Keras/TensorFlow framework, Adam Optimizer (0.0001 learning rate) executed on an Nvidia GeForce GTX 1080 Ti GPU environment.
Dataset Used: National Hospital Ambulatory Medical Care Survey (NHAMCS) open dataset comprising 118,602 patient visits to United States EDs (2012–2016 survey cycles).
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. Predictive Accuracy: The custom 2D data-to-image CNN classifier achieves a robust Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) score of 0.86 and a global accuracy of 0.77.2. Class Imbalance Rectification: Implementing random under-sampling combined with loss-function class-weight adjustments successfully stops the deep network from ignoring the minority admission class.3. Feature Priority: Baseline ML evaluations confirm that triage-stage variables (age, arrival time, blood pressure, respiratory rate, and pain scale) hold significant distinct predictive signatures for downstream admission.
Limitations: The five-level ESI scale causes massive crowding in Level 3, grouping vastly different severities together; heavily dependent on subjective nurse inputs.
Future Scope: Enhancing the system with hospital-specific real-time operational deployment and expanding the feature matrix to incorporate unstructured narrative chief complaint logs.

Paper Name: Deep Learning to Predict Hospitalization at Triage: Integration of Structured Data and Unstructured Text
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: Dual-channel parallel multimodal deep learning architecture merging structured indicators and free-text notes.• Structured Channel: Standard Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) mapping 15 numeric and 9 categorical fields with min-max scaling and one-hot encoding.• Text Channel: Natural Language Processing (NLP) pipeline executing case conversion, French language SnowballStemmer word roots, tokenization, and a Keras embedding layer returning dense vectors.• Fusion Module Stack: 1D Convolutional Neural Network (Conv1D) layers operating on embedded words, global max-pooling 1D blocks, output concatenation layer, and a final classification MLP.• Tech Stack: Python, NLTK, Pandas, Scikit-Learn, Keras.
Dataset Used: Extensive institutional dataset from Amiens-Picardy University Hospital (APUH) in France, compiling 260,000 ED records across more than 4 years (2015–2019).
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. Multimodal Performance Boost: The dual-input text-plus-tabular deep architecture locks in a strong predictive accuracy with a ROC-AUC of 0.83.2. High Admission Threshold: Comprehensive metric statistics demonstrate that approximately 35% of all incoming triage encounters over the 4-year span result in hospital admission.3. Length of Stay Disparity: Data analytics reveal that discharged patients spend an average of 4h04m in the ED, which is exactly 18% lower than the stay duration of admitted patients.
Limitations: Roughly 70,000 patient records suffered from entirely blank or missing textual triage note fields, introducing data sparsity challenges.
Future Scope: Validating the multimodal network architecture in real-time French clinical triage workflows and testing advanced context-aware Transformer blocks to extract hidden language patterns.

Paper Name: Dynamic Dempster Multi-Layer Perceptron for the prediction of admission patient in emergency department
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: Dynamic Dempster Multi-Layer Perceptron (DDMLP) integrating the Dempster-Shafer (D-S) mathematical theory of evidence with neural network backpropagation to model medical data uncertainty.• Forward Optimization Modules: Flexible input layer that dynamically alters neuron counts based on active, non-empty parameters per patient case.• Uncertainty & Weight Logic: Computes a Basic Belief Assignment (BBA) mass function for every incoming data variable using Dempster's rule of combination to systematically handle missing data points.• Network Weights Module: Converts computed BBAs into explicit Weight Belief Assignments (WBA) to dynamically guide signal propagation.• Backward Learning: Implements standard Back-Propagation (BP) to fine-tune network parameters during the training phase.
Dataset Used: Real-world triage data provided by the Lille University Hospital Center (LUHC) in France under the national Inter and Intra Hospital Logistics Optimization project.
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. Uncertainty Mitigation: The mathematical integration of D-S theory allows the system to systematically track and filter out high-conflict or incomplete medical evidence.2. Reduced Architectural Complexity: By completely ignoring inactive neurons that contain missing values, the dynamic framework significantly drops computational complexity and training time compared to standard MLPs.3. Intelligent Initialization: Calculating the early node connection weights via the WBA equation guides the backpropagation algorithm significantly faster than traditional random weight initialization.
Limitations: The system's underlying normalized combination rule loses effectiveness if there is an exceptionally high degree of semantic conflict between data sources.
Future Scope: Scaling the DDMLP approach to comprehensively explore deep cross-correlations between distinct pairs of medical variables instead of assuming absolute feature independence.

Paper Name: Explainable AI for Trustworthy Clinical Decision Support: A Case-Based Reasoning System for Nursing Assistants
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) merging Post-Hoc Explainable AI (XAI) with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).• Data Ingestion Module: De-identification protocol pipeline stripping personal information to maintain strict HIPAA privacy compliance.• Vector Database Stack: High-dimensional vector indexing database built using Facebook AI Similarity Search (FAISS) for lightning-fast sub-millisecond retrieval.• RAG Retrieval Engine: OpenAIEmbeddings processing model combined with a dual-path retrieval pipeline that cross-references real-time parameters with structured clinical case guidelines.• XAI Interface Module: Logic-tracing module that outputs historical case similarities and reasoning texts to validate the system's suggestions to staff.
Dataset Used: High-dimensional simulation dataset combining medical knowledge repositories, nursing case reports, licensing exam questions, and simulated wearable vitals.
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. Cognitive Load Alleviation: Multi-case simulations confirm that providing instant, evidence-grounded recommendations significantly drops cognitive load for frontline nursing assistants.2. Dynamic Logic Tracing: The XAI module establishes clear accountability by explicitly tracing a recommendation (e.g., fluid restriction in heart failure) back to matched past case successes.3. Multi-Source Data Synchronization: The FAISS vector database smoothly unifies highly volatile real-time streaming wearable sensor data (vitals) with static text guidelines.
Limitations: The framework is preliminary and has only been thoroughly vetted through technical simulations rather than undergoing true live clinical field deployment.
Future Scope: Scaling the system's vector retrieval capabilities to safely handle highly intricate, multi-condition patient profiles with intersecting chronic diagnoses.

Paper Name: Explainable NLP Model for Predicting Patient Admissions at Emergency Department Using Triage Notes
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: Transformer-based Natural Language Processing (NLP) text classifier combined with post-hoc model-agnostic interpretability tools.• Transformer Module: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) language model architecture fine-tuned specifically for emergency domain text.• Explainability Stack: Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) framework applied to individual textual inference calls.• Feature isolation: Strict semantic-only modeling pipeline that fully drops all traditional numeric and categorical fields to force the system to learn exclusively from narrative syntax.• Tech Stack: Python, Transformers library, Hugging Face repository, ktrain low-code library wrapper, and the Keras framework.
Dataset Used: Anonymized clinical dataset from Amiens-Picardy University Hospital in France containing over 260,000 ED visits spanning more than 4 years, approved under Hors Loi Jarde protocol PI2019 843 0066.
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. High Visual Interpretability: Integrating the post-hoc LIME architecture allows the system to systematically highlight the exact words or phrase structures that trigger an admission decision.2. Language Mapping Competence: Fine-tuned BERT embeddings successfully map complex, multi-word French clinical narratives of identical underlying medical meaning into tight vector clusters.3. Specialty Granularity: Beyond basic binary admission prediction, the data notes capture and group distinct target specialties into clear blocks: short-term care (42.4%), medical specialty (33%), and surgery (19.7%).
Limitations: The focus on explainability means the text-only framework deliberately sacrifices minor marginal performance gains that could be achieved with tabular variables.
Future Scope: Conducting full clinical utility testing in an active emergency room environment to quantify how much visible word-highlighting field maps improve physician trust.

Paper Name: Improving Emergency Triage Accuracy with Gradient Boosting Algorithms and NLP-Derived Features
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: Text-driven multi-class gradient boosting classification framework.• Preprocessing Module: Custom dictionary containing 150 medical abbreviations mapping acronyms to expanded syntax via regular expressions, combined with lowercase translation, punctuation stripping, and SpaCy lemmatization (es_core_news_sm).• Vectorization Stack: Word2Vec model running a highly parallelized Skip-gram architecture to build a continuous multidimensional semantic space.• Optimization & Balancing Modules: Aquila Optimizer swarm-intelligence metaheuristic engine automatically tuning hyperparameters, paired with a Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) balancing loop.• Classification Algorithms: CatBoost (Categorical Boosting), XGBoost, and LightGBM.
Dataset Used: Real-world clinical dataset from the Hospital Regional de Talca emergency department in Chile, tracking adult patient records over a 1-year period (2016–2017).
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. CatBoost Algorithm Victory: The custom-tuned CatBoost model outpaces alternative algorithms, securing a multi-class triage accuracy score of 0.733 and a weighted F1-score of 0.731.2. Semantic Clustering Verity: t-SNE dimensionality projections prove the optimized Word2Vec engine successfully groups highly variable unstandardized clinical text into cohesive groups.3. Extreme Class Rectification: Standardizing synthetic SMOTE caps (10,000 instances for micro-classes; 15,000 for mid-classes) effectively shields the models from severe baseline frequency skewing.
Limitations: Extreme triage labels (C1 vital emergencies and C5 non-emergencies) collectively represent less than 2% of real-world data, creating severe training sample sparsity.
Future Scope: Integrating structural vital sign streams alongside the Word2Vec text matrices to construct an fully unified multimodal local triage support system.

Paper Name: Interpretable Machine Learning for Emergency Department Triage: A Systematic Literature Review
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: Systematic Literature Review (SLR) evaluating the global landscape of Interpretable Machine Learning (IML) applied to emergency triage.• Methodological Modules: PRISMA 2020 protocol mapping, PICOC scope constraint tracking, and automated relational keyword Boolean searching.• Quality Evaluation Stack: 12-point quality checklist derived by explicitly modifying the MINIMAR reporting framework for ML and CONSORT-AI clinical trial guidelines.• Analytics Categorization: Grouping platforms into post-hoc explanation methods (SHAP, LIME) versus inherently transparent scoring models (AutoScore, SERP).
Dataset Used: Multi-database metanalysis filtering 362 initial search results down to 25 core peer-reviewed journal studies across Scopus, IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, PubMed, ScienceDirect, and Springer.
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. Dominant High-Performance Range: Across the entire reviewed global IML domain, validated models consistently deliver high prediction accuracies, with AUROC metrics tightly bounded between 0.78 and 0.96.2. Algorithmic Seniority: Gradient boosting variants (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) combined with post-hoc SHAP metrics function as the most popular framework configuration.3. AutoScore Proportional Trade-Off: Inherently transparent systems like AutoScore score slightly lower on pure AUROC (averaging ~0.88) but represent the gold standard for effortless medical readability.
Limitations: High reliance on structured EHR entries leaves narrative data underutilized; data is heavily biased toward high-income countries, lacking representation for LMICs.
Future Scope: Shifting research models from pure clinical survival outputs toward operational workflow metrics like patient waiting delays and staff allocation matrices.

Paper Name: M³Stroke: Multi-Modal Mobile AI for Emergency Triage of Mild to Moderate Acute Strokes
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: Non-invasive, on-device audio-visual multi-modal fusion mobile AI screening engine (M³Encoder).• Video Branch: Square face localization cropping bounding boxes via the Apple Vision framework, feeding frame sequences into a ResNet-34 frame encoder pre-trained on the FairFace dataset.• Audio Branch Stack: Dual tracking consisting of a ResNet-18 Local Audio Encoder (ESC-50 pre-trained) for fine details, and an Audio Spectrogram Transformer (AST) for global spectrogram attention.• Sequence Modeling Module: Structured State-Space sequence model (S4) utilizing High-Order Polynomial Projection Operator (HiPPO) theory to compute long temporal patterns at linear O(L) efficiency.• Loss Stack: Adversarial loss coupled with Mean Squared Error (MSE) within a Joint Identity Disentangle module to strip facial identity features from the network.• Tech Stack: iOS app deployed offline on iPad Pro M-series chips using native on-board computing.
Dataset Used: Institutional clinical dataset containing 269 emergency patients suspected of acute stroke (191 stroke-positive confirmed via diffusion-weighted MRI; 78 stroke mimics).
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. Outperforming Clinicians: The mobile application drastically beats standard clinical nurse triage, delivering a 16.82% increase in accuracy, 20.44% higher sensitivity, and a 14.29% gain in specificity.2. Operational Speed Execution: The optimized, optical-flow-free mathematical formulation achieves full speech recording, pre-processing, and inference fully on-device within 3 minutes.3. S4 Sequence Modeling Success: Harnessing the S4 structured state-space sequence design completely bypasses the gradient breakdown limitations of RNNs/LSTMs over long face videos.
Limitations: Facial cosmetics, highly complex irregular multi-person backgrounds, or ambient lighting changes can interfere with delicate spatiotemporal micro-expression extraction.
Future Scope: Initiating multi-center emergency transportation ambulance field trials, expanding self-monitoring tele-stroke services, and proving demographic algorithmic fairness.

Paper Name: Machine Learning-Based Pre-Diagnosis Tools in Emergency Departments: Predicting Hospitalization, Mortality and Triage Acuity
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: Feature-engineered multi-task pre-diagnostic machine learning classification pipeline.• Pre-processing Module: Multivariate Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE) to iteratively fill missing values, coupled with clinical outlier thresholds and categorical mode mapping.• Feature Extraction Block: Polynomial Features (PF) transformation module designed to explicitly map intricate non-linear cross-multiplication interactions between variables.• Dimensionality Reduction: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to condense the exploded polynomial feature space into linear principal components.• Classification Classifiers Stack: Random Forest (RF), Logistic Regression (LR), and Decision Tree (DT) classifiers evaluated across multi-task binary and multiclass configurations.
Dataset Used: High-fidelity clinical subsets extracted from the Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care IV (MIMIC-IV) and MIMIC-IV ED databases, profiling up to 200,000 samples across 95 variables.
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. Peak Predictive Accuracies: The polynomial feature-engineered pipeline secures top-tier accuracy metrics: 97.00% for binary hospitalization, 86.41% for mortality, and 99.80% for ESI triage acuity.2. Non-Linear Interdependency Capture: Incorporating the custom Polynomial Features transformation step discovers highly vital non-linear cross-interactions among clinical vitals that standard ML pipelines miss.3. PCA Interpretation Clarity: Running a final PCA stage effectively compresses high-dimensional variables down to essential components, optimizing data storage and system speed.
Limitations: Variables containing more than 30% missing values have to be fully dropped, which can filter out potentially valuable clinical indicators.
Future Scope: Designing an active e-triage software module to display real-time interactive risk alert bars for emergency nurses during high-volume intake hours.

Paper Name: NLP-Based Prediction of Medical Specialties at Hospital Admission Using Triage Notes
Methodology / Approach (Modules, Methods: • Core Technology: Dual-channel parallel deep learning framework integrating standard tabular indicators with narrative text.• Tabular MLP Channel: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) mapping 9 categorical and 15 numeric triage fields using one-hot matrix formatting and linear scaling.• Text CNN Channel: French language NLP pipeline executing token merging, NLTK-driven lowercase normalization, French SnowballStemmer mapping, vectorization, and dense word embeddings feeding parallel 1D CNN filtering channels.• Aggregation Stack: Mid-level feature fusion module that concatenates the output vectors of both the tabular MLP and text CNN into a unified global feature map.• Tech Stack: Python, Pandas, Scikit-Learn, NLTK, Pyspellchecker, and the Keras deep learning framework.
Dataset Used: Institutional administrative dataset provided by the Amiens-Picardy University Hospital (APUH) in France, capturing 260,000 individual ED encounters (2015–2019).
Key Findings (At least 3 main findings): 1. Early Specialty Routing: The model accurately maps incoming triage text directly to three major downstream clinical destinations: Short-Term Care Units (42.4%), Medical Specialties (33%), and Surgical Departments (19.7%).2. French Language Customization: Successfully implements native French text preprocessing (including pyspellchecker validations) to process non-English triage data without translation layer loss.3. Feature Map Cohesion: Merging the multi-channel Conv1D text outputs directly with the tabular layer's dense vectors delivers significantly higher accuracy metrics than running text or numbers in isolation.
Limitations: Missing or entirely blank narrative nursing notes across approximately 70,000 patient rows created massive gaps in the text channel data.
Future Scope: Replacing the baseline parallel 1D CNN architecture with state-of-the-art multi-head attention Transformer networks to further capture long-distance sentence contexts.
